data.title: Diebold Nixdorf: Index Inclusion Is Additional Validation Of Diebold Nixdorf's Turnaround
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis of Diebold Nixdorf (DBD) that was published on September 23. I do not often write about companies in the tech sector, but DBD’s operations are connected to banking and financial services, two industries which I have covered in detail over the last year in Seeking Alpha. I rated Diebold Nixdorf as a Buy in my initial analysis, and the stock has gained almost 40% in about six and half months of time, outpacing the S&P 500 by over 37%. As part of the support for my previous rating, I noted that the company’s profitability, free cash flow, and profit margins were all trending in the right direction. DBD was also making progress on reducing its debt load which would help reduce future interest costs.

On April 7, Diebold Nixdorf announced that it would be added to the S&P SmallCap 600 Index on April 10. This has given the stock an addition boost this week as it creates buying pressure for index funds who now need to add DBD to their holdings. Fundamentally, this change does little for Diebold Nixdorf except give additional validity to a company that declared bankruptcy less than three years ago.

The better news for DBD shareholders is that progress continues to be made with the company’s operations, and expectations for FY 2026 seem to be getting stronger. When I last wrote about the company, earnings per share for this year were expected to be around $4.96. In just over half a year’s time, that number now stands at $5.49. Even with this week's index-related price spike, the stock still trades at a forward PE below 15. I think this week’s run-up in price may settle back down in the next week or so, but over the long-term, Diebold Nixdorf still looks like a buy.

Company Overview

Diebold Nixdorf is a retail technology company that has a history dating back over 160 years. The company’s first product was a safe, and today’s version of DBD is still heavily connected to safety and security. Perhaps best known for its production of ATM machines that started in the 1970’s, Diebold Nixdorf has also been connected to the start of drive-up banking, the first kiosk in quick service restaurants, and the first circular vault door. For more detail about the history of the company and its operations, readers are encouraged to look back at my first article from last September.

The current version of Diebold Nixdorf serves both the banking and retail industries with hardware and software to enhance customer experience and security. DBD has a presence in over 100 countries worldwide and employs approximately 20,000 people. The company reported $3.80 billion in revenue in FY 2025, a 1.5% increase from the previous year. A breakdown in the sources for that revenue is given below. As can be seen, the banking side of the business produces the largest share of DBD’s top line.

The company also reported a backlog of orders at the end of last year that totaled $733 million, a decrease of 7.2% from the end of 2024. This total includes product orders that are projected to be shipped or installed in the next 18 months. The backlog total does not include any advanced orders for its services business, which makes this piece of information somewhat less valuable for predicting exactly what the future for the company may hold.

At the end of 2025, Diebold Nixdorf reported total assets of $3.85 billion on its balance sheet, 8.8% more than at the end of 2024. Stockholders’ equity rose 18.3% in 2025 from $929.8 million to $1.10 billion. The total net income for the company last year equaled $94.6 million, which is a complete reversal from the $16.5 million that was lost in FY 2024.

DBD does not pay a dividend and stated in its last 10-K that it is unlikely to initiate any payouts in the near future. However, the company has been active in repurchasing shares over the last year. In February of 2025, the board authorized a $100 million stock buyback plan. In November of last year, it approved a program to repurchase an additional $200 million. This is not insignificant for a company that has a total market cap of just $2.7 billion. During FY 2025, DBD repurchased just over 2.3 million shares at a value of $128 million. The buyback activity for Q4 2025 is shown in the table below.

The Case for Diebold Nixdorf

There was a lot of good news to share in Diebold Nixdorf's Q4 2025 earnings call. In addition to the increase in revenue, the company reported a significant increase in both EBITDA and EBITDA margin on a year-over-year basis. EBITDA moved up 7.4% to $485 million, while EBITDA margin rose from 12.1% to 12.7%. Free cash flow for the year increased a whopping 120% as Q4 was the fifth consecutive quarter of positive free cash flow, a record for the company.

All of these items helped spur DBD to a substantial increase in its adjusted earnings per share. However, the 182% improvement shown in the graph above from $1.98 per share to $5.59 per share does come with a footnote. The amount reported included $1.08 of non-cash operational tax benefits. Nonetheless, even if those are pulled out, the remaining total of $4.51 is well above the $3.56 that was expected for the company when I wrote my last analysis last September.

Other financial trends for the company appear to be largely positive. Total revenue crossed the $1 billion mark in Q4 2025 and trended upward throughout the year. Diebold Nixdorf noted in its financial filings that it normally books about 45% of its revenue in the first half of the year, so increases in the second half are expected. Gross margins also trended upward last year, as the graph below shows. Operating expenses remained relatively constant over the last several quarters with operating expense as a percent of revenue dropping 1.2% from Q4 2024 to Q4 2025.

There are a number of reasons to believe that FY 2026 will look even better than FY 2025 for DBD. The company reported that it has built momentum in its banking operations with solutions that drive efficiency for local bank branches such as teller cash recyclers and fit-for-purpose ATMs that can be customized to meet the needs of the specific location. One of my initial concerns for the company was that digital payments and a push toward more cashless transactions could impact demand for ATMs, but the market for those devices is expected to rise in the US, albeit modestly, through 2033.

On the retail side, Diebold Nixdorf's new AI-powered solution, "Vynamic Smart Vision I Shrink Reduction" satisfies retailers' need to implement and deploy new technologies across multiple store locations and geographies. The software can monitor self-checkout lines and regular shopping lanes for mistakes and theft-like behavior. This saves businesses that use the software both time and money in the long run. This example is just one of the ways that the company has evolved to meet the needs of the retail industry and provide additional support for my Buy recommendation.

Risks to Consider

The debt load for Diebold Nixdorf is still substantial. Long-term debt on the company’s balance sheet at the end of 2025 totaled $938.5 million. The good news is that DBD’s ability to manage this debt and the costs associated with it seem to be improving. On September 19, Diebold Nixdorf received a credit upgrade from S&P Global Ratings from “B” to “B+”. In mid-December of last year, Moody's revised its rating from "B2" to "B1" with a stable outlook. While these improvements are a positive sign, the current ratings still place the company’s debt in categories considered highly speculative. Interest expenses pose a significant challenge to DBD’s long-term profitability. Investors are reasonable to proceed with caution on any investment in the company considering it filed for bankruptcy less than three years ago. Any slip up in the company’s recovery could quickly erode investor confidence in the stock.

Diebold Nixdorf operates in competitive market segments that depend on a healthy banking sector for orders for hardware and additional services. Declines in profits from banks, either in the US or overseas, could cause them to delay upgrading their assets and negatively impact DBD’s bottom line. Pricing competition could also affect profit margins for the company that have been trending positively over the last year. Diebold Nixdorf will have to continue to innovate both its hardware and software to stay competitive both in the US and overseas.

The company’s operations in foreign countries also exposes it to some currency risk. DBD does use derivatives to manage some risk that involves trade and investments in foreign subsidies. Diebold Nixdorf reports its earnings in US dollars, which has gained some traction in 2026 after dropping roughly 10% in 2025. DBD lost $44.1 million in currency exchange in FY 2025 after reporting a gain of $13.8 million in 2024.

Looking Ahead to Q1 and FY 2026

Diebold Nixdorf is expected to release its Q1 2026 earnings on May 7. The average estimate from the three analysts that cover the company calls for adjusted earnings per share of $0.64. That would be a significant improvement from the $0.09 that was reported in Q1 2025.

From my perspective, an earnings miss for the first three months of this year would not be a total surprise. The $0.09 from Q1 last year was $0.27 below expectations at that time. This year, supply chain issues and concerns around global trade that have arisen in the last month may have some impact on the company's top and bottom line. Continued margin expansion and positive free cash flow will be keys to DBD maintaining some of the momentum that it picked up at the end of last year. It is important to note that last year was an incredibly strong one for the company even with a weaker than expected Q1.

As part of its Q4 2025 earnings presentation, Diebold Nixdorf released the following expectations for FY 2026, and all were improvements from the company’s last forward update from an investor presentation made last year. Revenue is expected to be roughly $3.9 billion, an increase of about 2.6% from FY 2025. Free cash flow is expected to be in a range of $255 million to $270 million as DBD targets a 50% conversion rate or better from its EBITDA. Its range of adjusted earnings is projected to average $5.50 per share, a 22% improvement from FY 2025 once its tax benefits are removed.

Conclusion

Diebold Nixdorf has been on an incredibly strong run over the last year with its stock up almost 130% in the last 12 months. However, at its current price, DBD is a tech stock with a forward PE below 15 and a PEG ratio well below 1. This still sounds like a good deal to me and a reason to maintain my Buy rating.

data.title: ASML Q1 Earnings Preview: Strong Growth Tailwinds, But Valuation Stretched
data.description:
Article Thesis

ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) will report its next earnings results on April 15. In this article, I will take a look at what investors can expect from the upcoming report. ASML remains a company with nice growth tailwinds, but following huge gains over the last year, shares are rather pricey now, which is why I don't think ASML is a great investment at north of $1,400 per share.

Past Coverage

I have written about ASML Holding here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in January, when I published this article. I gave the company a neutral rating then, which has worked out well so far -- at the time of writing, the total return since that article was published around two and a half months ago is exactly zero. Earlier last year, I had given ASML a Buy rating, with strong gains since then, but I eventually switched to a Neutral rating as ASML got more expensive, which weakened its total return outlook to some degree. With ASML's next earnings coming up, I want to update my views on the company and take a look at what we can expect from the next earnings report.

ASML Q1 Earnings Report: What Can We Expect?

The chip industry continues to boom on the back of strong AI investments that cause NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Micron (MU), and so on to experience huge revenue growth. Higher sales by these companies and their peers mean that more chips need to be produced, with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) being a key manufacturer, which in turn means that production capacity has to grow. That means more of ASML's lithography machines are needed -- so ASML is a clear beneficiary of the current AI investment wave as well. This is reflected in the current estimates for ASML's upcoming earnings report, as we can see here: Analysts believe that ASML will report a revenue increase of 14% to 15% next week, which is a pretty good growth rate, although less than the average over the last couple of quarters. It is, I believe, important to note that ASML tends to outperform expectations, though -- the company has beaten the revenue consensus estimate in 13 out of the last 16 quarters. History thus suggests that ASML will likely report a growth rate that is a little better than currently expected, although this isn't guaranteed, of course. Looking at estimates for the following quarters, we see that analysts believe that growth will accelerate, with Q2 revenue growth being forecasted at 18% and with Q3 revenue growth being forecasted at a very nice 29%, although this is partially due to a rather easy comparison due to Q3 2025 being a relatively weak quarter. Still, Q1 could be the very solid beginning of a year during which ASML will see some growth acceleration, which would be nice.

When it comes to profits, analysts are forecasting nice growth as well, although the expected earnings per share increase isn't outstanding, at 13% more compared to last year's first quarter. This is a little less than the expected revenue growth rate, which naturally isn't great -- ideally, we would want to see net profits grow a little faster than revenues due to margin increases thanks to operating leverage, with earnings per share growing a little faster than net income on top of that due to buybacks. But Wall Street analysts believe that some margin compression will cause ASML to report a slightly weaker earnings per share growth rate compared to its revenue growth rate. ASML has guided for a gross margin of 51% to 53% for the first quarter during the Q4 conference call -- at the midpoint of the guidance range, that's down 200 basis points from the 54% level seen in Q1 2025. It thus makes sense that analysts believe that margins could be lower in the upcoming report. ASML's management explained that their not very strong gross margin guidance was due to factors such as product mix and weaker expected servicing results. The good news is that management also explained that these factors wouldn't persist forever -- they see better margins in 2027, and their goal for 2030 is a gross margin of 57% at the midpoint, so meaningful improvements could be coming over the next couple of years. If that happens, ASML could see its earnings per share growth easily outpace its revenue growth beyond 2026. In the past, analysts oftentimes were too conservative with their earnings per share estimates, with 15 earnings per share consensus beats over the last 16 quarters. Like with ASML's revenues, we could thus see another beat when ASML reports next week, I think.

Investors also will be keen to hear what management has to say about Q2: The revenue guide will be important, and so will be ASML's gross margin guidance. Management may hint at gross margin improvements starting during 2026 already, which would be a nice development, although that is far from guaranteed -- it may take a couple more quarters until this metric improves meaningfully. Cash generation and usage will also be an important item to look for in the company's earnings report. ASML generated around $13 billion in free cash flow over the last year, so a little more than $3 billion per quarter. I expect that the Q1 report will show a nice cash flow number as well -- and maybe ASML has put that money to work via its $12 billion share repurchase program. While shares aren't cheap right now, which limits the effectiveness of share repurchases, the company has likely still bought back some shares on top of paying its recently increased dividend.

Is ASML Holding A Good Investment?

ASML is the clear leader in its industry, and global chip demand has been growing nicely for years. Unless the AI boom ends suddenly, chip demand will very likely grow in the coming years as well. This makes for a nice growth backdrop for ASML, I believe, but investors should, of course, not only consider a company's growth outlook.

Based on current estimates, ASML Holding trades for around 41x net earnings right now, resulting in an earnings yield of 2.4% or so. This makes for a rather pricey valuation, I believe, both in absolute terms and also compared to the valuations of many other growth companies with similar AI exposure: Broadcom, NVIDIA, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company all trade at considerably lower valuations compared to ASML. One can argue that ASML benefits from broad industry growth, no matter what company ultimately has the best chips, but that holds true for TSM as well -- and yet, TSM is way cheaper, trading at roughly 25x forward earnings.

Factoring in all of that, I'm neutral on ASML Holding for now. The company definitely has advantages such as its strong market position and positive growth outlook, but the stock looks fully priced -- I think upside potential is rather limited following the huge gains of almost 140% over the last year. It's better to buy into such stocks when they are comparatively inexpensive, such as last fall below $1,000, but I'm not interested in buying this close to the all-time high at a 40+ times earnings multiple.

data.title: Netflix Q1 Earnings Preview: With The WBD Saga Over, Focus Shifts To Advertising & Live Sports
data.description:
Introduction

Netflix Inc. (NASDAQ: (NFLX)) is scheduled to report its Q1 earnings on Thursday, 16 April. In this article, I explore the key factors that investors need to focus on in the company’s Q1 report.

A Recap of Q4 Results

NFLX had a strong finish to the year, comfortably beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $12.05 billion, up 17.6% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $83.91 million. Diluted EPS came in at $0.56, up 27.3% and beating analyst estimates by $0.01.

For the first quarter, management projected revenues to come in at $12.16 billion, which would translate to a growth of 15.3% y/y. Diluted EPS is expected to come in at $0.76, which would translate to a y/y growth of 15.1%. Finally, the company’s operating margins are projected to come in at 32.1%, which would represent an expansion of 40 bps y/y [or 0.4%].

Q1 Expectations

According to LSEG Data, analysts expect Q1 revenues to come in at $12.18 billion, which would represent a y/y growth of 16%. Non-GAAP EPS is expected to come in at $0.76, which would imply a y/y growth of 15%.

The company, according to LSEG data, has had a strong track record when it comes to beating revenue estimates, as evidenced by the fact that the stock has beaten revenue estimates in nine of the last ten quarters, with the magnitude of positive surprise coming in at a decent 0.63%, on average. Even in the quarter that it missed estimates, revenues were largely in line with analyst estimates. I expect NFLX to continue its track record of beating analyst estimates in Q1 given the strong content slate, driven by the likes of Season 7 of Black Mirror.

Now that there is clarity on that front, investor sentiment on the stock has largely turned positive. Having said that, with the stock still down 17% in the last six months, there is still a long way to go before the stock reaches its prior highs. Investor Focus Must Be on Ad Revenues & Live Sports

In NFLX’s Q4 letter to shareholders, management had talked in detail about the WBD acquisition and how it would help the company to “accelerate its business strategy.” Investors were less than pleased about the acquisition, as evidenced by the stock tumbling nearly 18% from the day the acquisition announcement was made until the day that the company announced that they were walking away.

Since the WBD saga came to an end, NFLX shares have surged, with a 10% surge immediately post the termination, once again highlighting just how relieved investors were that the company did not enter a bidding war with (PSKY).

Now that there is no acquisition-related overhang, the focus now shifts to the company’s core growth catalysts, which in my opinion, are advertising and its content & engagement.

With respect to advertising, investors, in my opinion, need to focus on whether revenues will maintain their trajectory towards achieving management’s target of approximately $3 billion for FY26, roughly double the figure achieved in 2025. Furthermore, investors should also focus on whether management offers revised targets for ad revenues in 2026, which would signal whether the company has corrected its course trajectory after a minor dip in Q4, as evidenced by the ad revenue growth of 79.1% y/y missing analyst expectations of 81.5% y/y. Any colour on the ad tier’s fill rates should also be watched by investors given that management is targeting a fill rate of approximately 70% in 2026, a significant jump from the 45% figure it achieved in 2025.

The growth in advertising revenues would also be reliant on how much progress NFLX makes on bringing more live sports to the platform. The company has already gained exclusive rights to the MLB’s Opening day, Home Run derby and the Field of Dreams games for the 2026 season and is looking to expand its relationship with NFL by adding more games ahead of the new season. The expansion of NFL partnership is key, in my opinion, to maintaining the growth trajectory of its advertising business. As such, management commentary on this aspect is yet another area that investors should focus on to gain more perspective about the long term growth prospects of the ads business.

Finally, while the company has once again raised prices across all its subscription plans last month for the first time since January 2025, the impact would not be felt at least until the second quarter. However, it would also be vital for investors to see just how the company is planning to invest the additional revenues into its content, since this would also give them an idea about the trajectory of the ads business.

Valuation

I used a forward P/E multiple approach together with a bottom-up FY26 Profit & Loss build to compute my price target for NFLX. The valuation is directly tied to the thesis surrounding NFLX’s advertising business since the ads business is the primary growth catalyst for NFLX, in my opinion, going forward. I utilized NFLX management’s guidance and used the ad revenue growth assumption as the deciding variable across all three scenarios [bear, base, bull], in order to isolate the variable driving my investment thesis [ad revenue growth trajectory], while holding constant the company’s subscription business assumption.

NFLX management forecast FY26 revenues to come in between $50.70 and $51.70 billion [midpoint is $51.20 billion) and FY26 operating margins to come in at 31.5%. Critically, the operating margin guidance was made when NFLX was still in the running for WBD acquisition and it explicitly included about $275 million in acquisition-related expenses tied to WBD. Given that NFLX has walked away from the deal, the expenses no longer matter. Subsequently, adjusted FY26 operating margins would then be forecast to come in at approximately 32%.

NFLX management expect the company’s ad revenues to hit approximately $3 billion in FY26. I have assumed a 70% incremental operating margin on ad revenue after taking into account the fact the NFLX has already built its in-house Ads Suite infrastructure. This margin is held constant across all three scenarios.

Taking all of this together, the key inputs for my valuation computations are as follows:

I then constructed a 3x3 valuation matrix that combines the three FY26 EPS scenarios with the three forward P/E scenarios. The forward P/E multiples under each scenario are as follows:

Bear Case 25x: The multiple corresponding to NFLX’s 52-week low, reached during the WBD deal saga

The base case target of $99.20 are the levels around which NFLX is currently trading at. Moreover, the risk/reward ratio [base case upside/bear case downside] is nearly 0, far below the 1:1 threshold that signals accumulation.

Furthermore, when one looks at the tail asymmetry, it is interesting to note here that the bull case upside of about 23.9% is roughly symmetric with the bear case downside of 22.6%, which results in a tail R/R of approximately 1.06. This implies that NFLX stock heads into the earnings with a balanced risk profile with no directional skew to lean on pre-earnings.

Taking all of this together, the quant framework implies that NFLX is fairly valued at current levels. As such, I am downgrading my rating on the stock from a BUY to a HOLD.

I do want to add a caveat to my rating downgrade: it is not a reflection of my view on the long-term growth prospects of NFLX. On the contrary, as a NFLX shareholder, I continue to hold my shares and do believe that the fundamentals of the company look strong. The downgrade merely reflects the fact that at current levels, the stock is already pricing in the Q1 ad-revenue growth scenario, leaving no cushion for any disappointment. The risk/reward becomes more favourable at the mid-$80s. Alternatively, should the management revise its ad-revenue target to the upside, then the valuation framework needs to be visited as the R/R would become more favourable. For now, heading into the Q1 print, a HOLD rating is warranted.

Concluding Thoughts

It is set to be an interesting Q1 print from NFLX, the first since the post-WBD saga. The stock is entering the print with a strong momentum and as such, it is imperative that the company delivers a strong report, one that comfortably beats both the top- and bottom-line estimates.

The key focus area for investors, in my opinion, would be the progress made by the advertising segment, and whether the likes of MLB deals provide the management with a sufficient reason to revise their advertising revenue guidance to the upside. Any additional commentary on the NFL package would also help investors to have a sense of how the advertising revenue trajectory would look like in the long run.

From a valuation perspective, I am downgrading my rating from a BUY to a HOLD purely because my quant framework offers no meaningful cushion for disappointment. At current levels, the stock is fairly valued and given the positive momentum it has, any miss or downbeat guidance would be a headwind. For investors looking to enter the stock, I would either wait for a pullback to the mid-$80s or if the management revises their ad revenue targets upward in the Q1 print.

The WBD saga is over and NFLX investors are grateful for it. The focus should now be on whether the streaming giant can maintain its throne on its own, via advertising and live sports.

data.title: Stock Picks From Seeking Alpha's March 2026 New Analysts
data.description:
Showcase Intro

In March, we welcomed 21 new analysts who published their first-ever article on Seeking Alpha. In this article, our editors highlight some of the best ideas from these new analysts and introduce them.

The first five introductions are spotlight features, including a longer excerpt from the analyst's article. The rest of the new analysts will be introduced after these with a more brief excerpt and are generally organized by rating from Strong Sell to Strong Buy. Each section includes details about the new analysts' interests and backgrounds, so you can get to know them a bit more.

With all the ideas and information shared in this article, we'd invite you to join the conversation and let others know what you think: are any of these picks worth following up on?

To our new analysts: welcome to the community! And please don't hesitate to share more in the comments to introduce yourself to our readers.

Top Stock Picks From Seeking Alpha's New Analysts

data.title: Magnolia Oil & Gas: Over $700 Million In Projected FCF At Current 2026 Strip
data.description:
Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY) was unhedged at last report, allowing it to fully benefit from higher oil prices. While Magnolia's oil cut is projected to be around 39% in 2026, it also has a substantial amount of NGLs (with prices that have also benefitted from recent events) and has a liquids percentage around 68%.

Thus, my projection of Magnolia's 2026 free cash flow has improved from around $450 million a couple of months ago to over $700 million now.

Much of Magnolia's free cash flow is expected to go towards share repurchases. Magnolia's share price has increased since I last looked at it, but by a substantially lower percentage than its projected 2026 free cash flow has increased. This may allow it to increase its rate of share repurchases (using up its current share repurchase authorization during 2026) and still have a couple hundred million left over from its 2026 free cash flow.

I have increased my estimate of Magnolia's value from $26 to $31 per share due to its higher projected 2026 free cash flow as well as a $5 increase in my long-term WTI oil price (from $70 to $75)

2026 Outlook At $80 WTI oil

Magnolia's 2026 production is expected to be around 105,000 BOEPD, with a high-30s oil cut.

The current strip includes roughly $80 WTI oil and $3.65 NYMEX natural gas, with front-month WTI futures in the mid-$90s at the moment (although quite volatile).

Magnolia is projected to generate $1.635 billion in revenues at those commodity prices.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 14,873,750 $78.50 $1,168
NGLs 11,223,750 $22.00 $247
Gas 73,365,000 $3.00 $220
Total Revenues $1,635

Magnolia previously indicated that its income taxes for 2026 were expected to be entirely deferred. That was at a time when the 2026 WTI strip was in the low $60s, though, so I've assumed a minor ($15 million) amount of cash income taxes at these higher current strip prices.

$ Million
Lease Operating $192
Gathering, Transportation, and Processing $77
Taxes Other Than Income $96
Cash G&A $70
Net Cash Interest $15
Capex $460
Cash Income Taxes $15
Total $925

Magnolia is thus projected to generate $710 million in 2026 free cash flow now, leaving it with approximately $590 million in free cash flow after dividends.

Share Repurchases

Magnolia returns much of its free cash flow to shareholders in the form of share repurchases. It noted that it returned 75% of its 2025 free cash flow to shareholders in the form of approximately $322 million in share repurchases and dividends. Of that $322 million, 64% (or $205.5 million) went towards share repurchases. Magnolia repurchased 8.9 million shares during 2025, reducing its diluted weighted average total shares outstanding by 4.4%.

Magnolia's total remaining share repurchase authorization was last reported at 12.9 million common shares, after its board increased the existing authorization by 10 million shares.

At around $30 per share, Magnolia could repurchase 12.9 million shares during 2026 and still have around $200 million left over for other purposes.

Notes on Valuation

I previously estimated Magnolia's value at $26 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario. I've increased that estimate to $31 per share now.

The increase reflects a combination of higher 2026 oil prices plus a $5 increase in my long-term oil price (to $75).

Compared to when I last looked at Magnolia, its projected 2026 free cash flow has improved by around $1.30 per share (or approximately $250 million). This helps increase the potential quantity of share repurchases despite its somewhat higher share price.

I've also bumped up my long-term oil price from $70 to $75 due to increased geopolitical risk. At $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, Magnolia should be able to generate over $650 million in free cash flow (before cash income taxes) while maintaining oil production levels.

Conclusion

Although Magnolia's 2026 oil cut is expected to be below 40%, its liquids percentage is nearly 70%. With its lack of hedges, it benefits significantly from strong oil prices, and thus I now project that it can generate over $700 million in 2026 free cash flow at current strip prices. This is a roughly $250 million improvement compared to when I looked at Magnolia in early February.

Magnolia could potentially repurchase over 10 million shares during 2026 while also having hundreds of millions left over (from its 2026 free cash flow) after those share repurchases.

As a result of strong near-term oil prices plus an increase in my long-term oil price, I now estimate Magnolia's value at $31 per share. This is up from my previous $26 per share estimate.

data.title: CNY At A Glance: China's Yuan Moves Into Our Bullish Scenario
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

CNY has outperformed most currencies vis-a-vis the dollar this year

The Chinese yuan has tended to fall in the middle of the pack in terms of its performance versus the US dollar in the past few years, as the currency stability objective of the People's Bank of China has led to very limited movement in the yuan.

This has changed so far in 2026. Year-to-date, the CNY has actually been one of the notable outperformers against the USD, up 2.3%, easily outperforming most other Asian currencies on the year. Since the outbreak of the Iran war to the time of writing, the yuan (CNY) and the offshore yuan (CNH) are the only currencies in our tracked basket that have actually gained against the USD.

So what's the story behind this surprising outperformance?

  • First, the market's bullish sentiment has carried forward. We wrote about the building bullish sentiment in the last update of this report in January. In our conversations with a wide range of market participants, we note that this bullish sentiment on the CNY remains intact and seemingly quite widespread.
  • Second, policymakers appear content to allow for further CNY strengthening. There was some question about this when policymakers cut the foreign exchange risk reserve ratio from 20% to 0% in what was an apparent attempt to halt the pace of CNY appreciation. However, after the outbreak of the Iran war, and the immediate spike in oil prices, there appeared to be a change of heart, as the PBoC's daily fixings suggested a tolerance for further appreciation.
  • Third, macro level drivers continue to suggest room for further appreciation of the CNY versus the USD if the situation in the Middle East is brought under control. China's exports are off to another strong start this year, suggesting that the current account surplus is going to remain a positive factor for the yuan. Meanwhile, the currency has largely weathered the widening US–China yield spread, which should resume its narrowing trend if inflation expectations come under control. That is far from guaranteed. Since the outbreak of the war, two dominant narratives have emerged: warnings from experts that markets may be underestimating the impact, and a widely held expectation among investors of a TACO (‘Trump always chickens out’) scenario and eventual normalisation.
  • Fourth, the CNY has been a surprising winner of the Iran war, despite China's role as the largest oil importer in the world. At least a few market participants have mentioned re-evaluating the "China risk premium" amid rising global uncertainty elsewhere, which has led to China looking more and more like the adult in the room. While the actual direct impact has been tangential at best, discussions of the potential damage to the petrodollar system and rise of the petroyuan – aided by Iran's plans to collect a fee for passage through the Strait of Hormuz to be collected in CNY or cryptocurrency – may have also added to longer-term bullish expectations for the CNY.

PBoC fixings suggest no opposition to gradual appreciation

In this report series, we've often talked about the PBoC's currency stability objective as one of the key deciding factors for the trajectory of the USD/CNY. The PBoC has exerted its influence on the currency primarily through its daily fixings. The counter-cyclical factor, which is the adjustment it makes to the daily fixing versus the previous day's market closing price, has been heavily relied on to curb excessive movements of the USD/CNY during periods of heavy market speculation. In the last few years, this has mostly been used to push back against heavy depreciation pressure. Towards the end of 2025, however, this pattern started to reverse, with the PBoC using fixings to push back more cautiously against appreciation.

The PBoC's counter-cyclical factor has been very close to neutral for most of March and the start of April, after leaning more forcefully against rapid appreciation in previous months. This has sent a signal that policymakers are open to the CNY continuing to strengthen, and remains one of the main factors supporting CNY appreciation. This was a little surprising as the counter-cyclical factor and the cutting of the foreign exchange risk reserve ratio from 20% to 0% had appeared to signal an effort to rein in CNY appreciation back in February.

The outbreak of the war in Iran looks to be the reason for the renewed embrace of a stronger CNY. China remains the world's largest oil importer, and while a slight appreciation of the CNY certainly won't offset the surge in oil prices, it can at least help mitigate the potential pain. Other factors may include the senior-level comments on attempting to balance trade and ramp up imports, to which a strong CNY would also contribute. Furthermore, it is possible that higher inflationary pressure and uncertainty from the war will delay potential PBoC rate cuts; as a result, we have pushed our forecasts back from 2Q26 to the second half of the year. Even though PBoC cuts tend to be modest, a delayed rate cut is nonetheless a positive factor for CNY appreciation.

CNY has shrugged off a sharp widening of yield spreads

US-China yield spreads have been a major driver of the USD/CNY's trajectory over the past few years. Higher US yields have been a key reason why Chinese exporters have kept a large share of their proceeds offshore, contributing to CNY weakness in recent years. However, as the Federal Reserve resumed its rate‑cutting cycle, the yield spread began a more predictable narrowing trend from early 2025 through early 2026.

The Iran war has quickly derailed this story, as US yields moved higher while Chinese yields edged a little lower. Over recent weeks, the two‑year US–China yield spread widened to its highest level since February 2025. Yet this translated into only a modest softening of the CNY over the same period, and by the time of writing, renewed prospects for peace have led markets to largely shrug off the wider yield spread entirely.

There's still a relatively high level of uncertainty on this front, mostly from the US side of the equation. If inflation remains under control, and the Fed eventually resumes rate cuts after a few pauses, we'll likely see a return of the narrowing yield spread story, which will further fuel CNY appreciation expectations. This is one of the reasons why the CNY has strengthened considerably on today's news of a two-week ceasefire.

However, if we move into a longer rate hold, or worse, a Fed rate hike environment where the yield spread stays elevated, the bullish case for the CNY begins to look weaker. This is a potential outcome of a longer war scenario, and remains worth monitoring.

CNY's recent outperformance across the board may not hold if peace is successful

We wrote earlier about the CNY's surprising outperformance against the USD since the start of the Iran war, beating the other currencies in our monitored basket.

As such, it should come as no surprise that the CFETS RMB index, which heavily weights the euro (17.9%), Korean won (8.5%), and Japanese yen (8.1%) alongside the US dollar (18.3%) has also strengthened noticeably in the same period. This index is up 2.3% since the start of the Iran war and 2.9% year-to-date.

While we are still optimistic about the CNY's prospects against the USD, the performance versus other pairs could lag behind by comparison. Other currencies, which have generally fared poorly after the Iran war, could bounce back more strongly, limiting the CNY’s relative scope for further appreciation. As such, the CFETS RMB index could underperform versus the USD if we see a scenario where the peace holds and the strong dollar backdrop fades.

Current account shows no sign of letting up so far in 2026

China has been running an especially strong current account surplus in the last couple of years, as external demand has been resilient despite the trade war. Despite facing a largely unfavourable base effect, China's trade growth appears to be off to another strong start this year. While imports look likely to rebound as well this year, China's trade surplus will remain very significant.

This is relevant for the CNY as the currency has shown clear historical correlation with the current account, as exporters tended to remit proceeds back into the CNY. This relationship appears to have broken down in recent years. While the current account balance has risen sharply, USD/CNY has remained largely range‑bound. This reflects exporters holding a greater share of foreign‑currency proceeds due to more attractive yields abroad relative to China, as well as generally subdued domestic investment appetite – underscored by the fact that 2025 marked the first year on record in which fixed‑asset investment contracted on an annual basis since data publication began.

What does this mean for the CNY? Assuming that exporters eventually begin to convert holdings back to the CNY – either to meet domestic obligations or as yield spreads narrow – this would translate into a lot of pent-up demand for the CNY. We have already observed this to be a factor during the CNY's rally to date, but considering the current levels for the CNY and the current account balance, it could be argued there's still plenty of room to go.

Rise of the 'petroyuan' remains a long-term theme rather than a short-term catalyst

Iran’s potential move to turn the Strait of Hormuz into a tolled passageway – requiring payments in cryptocurrency or CNY – has sparked lively debate about what this could mean for the yuan’s future. This includes renewed interest in the so‑called 'petroyuan', perhaps last discussed prominently near the start of the Russia–Ukraine war, when sanctions on Russia resulted in much of the country's oil trade with China moving to the CNY.

One interesting argument was that, if the Iran war were to end with Iran successfully controlling the Strait of Hormuz, this could lead to a system in which payments for passage are collected in CNY, and eventually to oil trade flows through the Strait and into Asia being denominated in CNY. In this scenario, the Iran war would be seen as dealing a major blow to the petrodollar system, with the CNY emerging as a key beneficiary amid an acceleration towards a 'petroyuan' system, and the world taking another step towards a more multipolar order.

It's not surprising why this topic has garnered interest and discussion. However, there are a few logical leaps that appear to occur here; it's assumed that the trade going through the Strait would be priced in CNY, rather than just the fee for passage. This would require the cooperation of all of the Gulf states, and there hasn't been anything to indicate that such a shift is currently in the works. It also assumes that Iran permanently assumes control of the Strait, and passing ships willingly pay the fees.

In the near term, the impact is likely to be very incremental. After all, there is not a strong level of clarity on Iran’s criteria for letting ships through the Strait of Hormuz, with only a small number of tankers currently permitted to transit. Among those ships that have secured passage, it is unclear whether or not a toll was paid, and whether the payments were made in CNY or other currencies.

In a thought exercise where we take the most optimistic assumptions, where traffic to the Strait of Hormuz is fully restored to around 100 vessels a day or 36k vessels a year, and that every single vessel that passed through the Strait ended up paying a USD 2mn equivalent fee in CNY, we'd still only get a somewhat paltry USD 72bn or RMB 491bn.

In a vacuum, this increases CNY demand and global usage. But how significant is that in the grand scale of things? Considering that China's Crossborder International Payments System (CIPS) handled around RMB 180tn of payments in 2025, an extra RMB 491bn wouldn't be negligible but far from a gamechanger.

Nonetheless, the fact that the CNY was requested as a payment currency certainly is something interesting to watch. It is a real-world example of the CNY's rising clout and a vote of support for China acting as a counterweight to the US-dominated financial system.

CNY's continued outperformance pushes us into the bullish forecast band

We are moving on from the previous baseline forecast fluctuation band of 6.85-7.25 for the USD/CNY, and lowering the new forecast fluctuation band to what was formerly our bullish scenario forecast of 6.70-7.05. We lower the year-end USD/CNY forecast to 6.75.

The CNY has performed stronger than expected after policymakers ended the pushback against appreciation and has largely shrugged off a strong dollar environment and elevated global uncertainty to continue its appreciation trajectory.

Risks to the band look roughly balanced at this point. Bullish factors remain in place, but markets also look largely positioned for this already, and it would still be a little surprising to see the PBoC tolerate the CNY strengthening past 6.70 this year; recall that the 6.70 level was basically the strongest for the CNY since currency stabilisation became a more prominent theme back in 2023. On the other hand, a bearish reversal past the top end of the band would likely require new catalysts such as a substantially more hawkish global central bank environment widening China's yield spreads versus other countries or a renewed pessimism setting in on China's growth. These are certainly possible if we see a strong inflationary impact from the war in Iran or if China's efforts to bolster domestic demand fall short.

The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Stock Outlook: Why The Bull Case Still Holds
data.description:
Morgan Stanley Overview

Morgan Stanley’s (MS) setup going into Q1 still looks good to me. I’m sticking with a Buy call for anyone thinking medium or longer term. The company’s wealth management arm keeps showing it can hold up through all kinds of cycles; they’re really good at using capital efficiently, and there’s some extra upside that might come from new rules or policies that Wall Street hasn’t really priced in yet. MS stock is up about 35% since I last called it a buy. I’m not bumping it up to Strong Buy, mainly because the stock has already run so far and there are still some risks around, nothing super scary, but not just background noise either. Still, I think there’s more room for the shares to climb, both from how the market values the business and what they can send back to shareholders, as Morgan Stanley keeps growing and their core earnings seem less tied to wild swings in the economy than most people expect.

Business Model Shift

The Morgan Stanley of today isn’t the same as the one before 2020. They now have $9.3 trillion in client assets, and wealth management makes up over 40% of their total revenue. The big shift is that they don’t rely as much on trading gains; now they get a steady stream of fees. In the past year, they pulled in $70.6 billion in net revenue, up 14% from the year before. Net income for common shareholders hit $16.2 billion, up 27%. Earnings per share came in at $10.21, a 28% jump. Their expense efficiency ratio got better, moving to 68% from 71%, even while they kept investing in growth, so cost control is real, not just something management talks about. Return on tangible common equity reached 21.6%, and ROE was 16.6%. Both numbers are in line with or better than what management is aiming for in the next few years. Their CET1 capital ratio is 15.0%, Tier 1 leverage is 6.7%, and supplementary leverage stands at 5.4%, all well above what regulators require. Book value per share is now $64.37, and tangible book value is up to $50.00. All of this shows the company isn’t just sturdy; they’re in a spot to put extra capital to work, either by growing the business or returning more to shareholders.

Wealth Engine Strength

Wealth Management sits at the center and keeps the whole thing steady. That part of the business brought in $31.8 billion in net revenue last year, up 21% from the year before, and pulled in $356 billion in new assets. Out of that, $160 billion came from fee-based asset flows. Pre-tax margin is sitting at 29.3%. They’re squeezing out more efficiency, even though costs haven’t budged much. Hitting a 30% pre-tax margin for Wealth Management isn’t just talk anymore, it’s close.

Institutional Rebound

Institutional Securities had a good year too, with $33.1 billion in net revenue, helped by clients getting more active on both the equity side and in advisory. Investment Banking brought in $7.6 billion, up 23%, thanks to more deals and more underwriting. These numbers tell you that MS can keep growing profits in all sorts of markets, not only when everyone’s feeling bullish.

Regulatory Uncertainty

Heading into Q1, things aren’t straightforward. The whole industry is in a slow shift, and regulation is right in the middle of it. Lately, there’s been talk that G-SIBs might get some regulatory breaks, which would give them more room for buybacks and bigger dividends. MS has been steadily growing its dividend; the quarterly payout has climbed from $0.35 in 2020 to $1.00 in 2025. That could pick up even more if capital rules get lighter. Management said before, “Our ROTCE goal is 20%... based on a normal market environment.” They’re already getting there, and any loosening of regulations would just add to that. None of this extra upside is really in consensus forecasts yet, or in most investors’ models.

MS Stock Valuation Setup

MS stock isn’t expensive right now considering its strengths. The shares go for about 15 times next year’s expected earnings, just a bit over 2.6 book value. MS’s forward price/earnings doesn’t really account for its steady high-teens ROTCE or the chance it could get a “compounder multiple” if it keeps pulling in big asset flows and keeps costs in check. If rules get easier and buybacks go up, it’s not a stretch to see the multiple move up to around 17–18x. Using next year’s estimated EPS of about $11.4, that would put the stock well over $190. If things go south, a recession hits, or asset growth slows down, the likely floor is somewhere between $100 and $110, based on tangible book and lower mid-cycle profits.

Risk

There’s one main variable: what happens with capital requirements and how clients feel. If expected changes to bank capital rules don’t end up benefiting large banks like Morgan Stanley, buybacks would still be limited and capital returns wouldn’t impress, which would take away a big reason for upside. If net new assets suddenly slow down—no signs of this right now, but it could happen if the economy takes a hit—both revenues and valuation would take a hit too. With all that, there’s nothing showing a fundamental problem; client assets are at all-time highs and capital ratios are well over the minimums, with a 320-basis-point CET1 cushion above what’s required.

Investors seem to be feeling a bit better lately after a stretch where people didn’t really pay much attention. UBS just bumped the stock up to buy, saying the recent drop opened up a decent chance for folks to get in. Most of the headlines now talk about what’s coming next, like potentially playing a role in SpaceX IPO access through E*Trade.

What To Expect From Morgan Stanley's Q1 Earnings

Q1 is shaping up to be about steady net inflows in Wealth Management, keeping pre-tax margins over 28%, and watching for early signs that management is getting more freedom to use capital, either by buying back shares or hinting at bigger dividends, as rules become clearer. If there’s going to be a big surprise, good or bad, it’ll probably come from these parts of the business, not from some one-off trading win or loss. Management’s track record has been pretty solid so far, delivering ROE of 16.6% and ROTCE of 21.6%.

I’m sticking with a Buy call, but not going all the way to Strong Buy. The company is building value, and the big risks seem to be under control, but for the stock to really move up a level, it’ll need some rule changes that aren’t here yet. The main reasons to stay positive are still intact: a strong capital base, high-margin recurring fees, and some real upside potential if things go right. The big run-up in the stock over the last year means you have to be careful about when you jump in. If client activity falls off, if expected changes to bank capital rules don’t end up benefiting large banks, or if credit losses suddenly spike, I’d have to rethink this view. For now, owning Morgan Stanley looks like a bet that the market isn’t giving enough credit to how steady its returns are, or to the chances it has to put its capital to work. The numbers back up a steady, incremental case, not one that relies on hope or big speculation.

data.title: My 2 Favorite Mispriced Dividends For Recurring Income
data.description:
Many investors say they’re looking for “value.” But in practice, most people are just looking for stocks that used to be more expensive and become cheap for no reason. That’s not the same thing.

The setups I care about tend to look uncomfortable at first glance. The market has a clear narrative. Something is “broken,” “peaking,” or “uncertain.” Multiples compress, sentiment fades, and the stock drifts into that category everyone avoids.

That’s where I start paying attention. What I’m really looking for is a mismatch between perception and reality, not perfection nor screaming growth. Quite simply, I want a business that’s better than the market thinks, with a path that’s more stable than the headline suggests.

Right now, two names I see that fit that framework in very different ways are Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) and Pfizer (PFE). They operate in completely different industries. However, the common thread is the same: both are being priced as if their best days are behind them.

In this article, I explore why I don’t think that’s true, and why they present amazing long-term opportunities, so let’s dig in!

Rexford Industrial Realty: Misunderstood Cyclicality in a Supply-Constrained Market

Rexford is a pure-play industrial REIT, which I last covered in October 2025. It’s focused almost entirely on infill Southern California. That makes it a niche player, but also a powerful one.

This isn’t generic warehouse exposure, but rather last-mile logistics in one of the most supply-constrained industrial markets in the country.

REXR operates in a market where land is scarce, entitlements are difficult, and replacement costs keeps rising. That creates a structural advantage that doesn’t show up clearly in short-term numbers.

So why is the stock cheap? It’s because the market thinks industrial rents have peaked. Over the past year, there’s been a growing narrative that the post-COVID surge in logistics demand is fading. Leasing spreads have been normalizing after the COVID surge. Development pipelines are catching up. And REITs, broadly, have been under pressure from higher interest rates.

Put all that together, and you get multiple compression. As shown below, REXR is trading at one of its widest discounts to normal valuation in history. At the current price of $34.23, it has a forward P/FFO of 14.4, sitting far below its normal P/FFO of 26.5.

But as I sift through the last earnings results, what stood out wasn’t weakness. It was resilience.

REXR saw embedded rent growth and total portfolio NOI rose by 5.7% during 2025. Even as market leasing activity moderates, Rexford is still rolling leases materially above prior levels, with same property cash NOI rising by 4.3% last yar.

I also find it encouraging that vacancy remains tight in their core market with occupancy at 96.4%. Southern California is supply constrained and population dense. This market isn’t easily replicated, and tenants still need proximity to population centers.

Moreover, REXR’s management is disciplined. They’re not chasing development aggressively at the wrong point in the cycle. They’re leaning into their value-add model. This involves acquiring underutilized properties and repositioning them to capture incremental value, as noted during the last earnings call:

Starting with repositioning and development, we expect to stabilize and commence rent on approximately 1.2 million square feet of value-added projects, generating $20 million of annualized NOI with the majority coming online by midyear. Conversely, approximately $12 million of annualized in-place NOI will come offline due to new construction starts, primarily related to our project at 9000 Airport Boulevard. The weighted average timing of the annualized NOI coming offline is late in the third quarter.

This has been in REXR’s playbook for years, and it tends to generate outsized returns over the long run without taking on excessive risk. REXR’s redevelopment pipeline is supported by a solid balance sheet with a net debt to enterprise value of just 25% and a safe net debt to EBITDAre ratio of 4.4x.

REXR currently yields 5.1% and the dividend is well-covered by a 73% payout ratio. Management apparently thinks the stock is too cheap, as it repurchased 6.3 million worth of stock last year at a weighted average price of $39.51.

Investors today get to buy REXR at an even lower price at $34.23 with a significant margin of safety. From the outside, it’s easy to say “industrial is slowing.” But from the inside, the business still looks like it has a long runway of internal growth in an attractive market. That disconnect is where the opportunity is on REXR at the moment and is a reason for why I’m so long-term bullish on the stock right now.

Pfizer: A Patent Cliff That’s Masking a Rebuild

Pfizer isn’t cheap because the market is confused. It’s cheap because the market sees a patent cliff coming. Over the next few years, several of Pfizer’s key drugs are losing exclusivity.

Management has been very clear about this. They’re staring down a real patent cliff between 2026 and 2028. This means revenue pressure is not hypothetical, but is already being modeled into the outlook. This year, PFE has $1.5 billion in revenue headwind from generic entry. That’s the kind of number that tends to result in multiples staying compressed, as in the case with PFE stock.

So, the market’s reaction makes sense on the surface. But here’s where I think things get more interesting. The market Is pricing the cliff, not the bridge. PFE’s latest Q4 2025 results showed revenue and EPS that exceeded expectations. Excluding Paxlovid and Comirnaty (COVID medications), revenue grew by 6% YoY for full year 2025, and by 9% YoY for Q4’25.

The next couple of years is a pivotal investment period as Pfizer is aggressively building its pipeline. In other words, earnings might look choppy in the short term as they’re spending to replace what’s being lost.

Management isn’t being passive on business development. The acquisitions of Seagen, Biohaven, and more recently Metsera are targeted bets to rebuild growth in oncology, neurology, and obesity. As shown below, these acquisitions bring a robust portfolio including leading oncology candidate PADCEV, a GLP-1 pill, and a leading migraine treatment.

PFE is supported by an ‘A’ rated balance sheet with a safe net debt to TTM EBITDA ratio of 2.1x. It currently yields 6.3% and the dividend is well-covered by a 53% payout ratio. As shown below, the dividend is very much ingrained in management’s plans for post-2028 growth (after key patent expirations).

At the current price of $27.47, I view PFE as being a bargain with a forward P/E of 9.3, sitting well under its historical P/E of 11.2. The bar is set quite low, for PFE. With multiple read-outs on late stage pipeline coming out this year and recently launched drugs showing double digit sales growth, I believe patient value investors could see strong total returns from here.

Risks to Consider

For Rexford, the biggest risk is a sharper-than-expected slowdown in leasing demand. If tenant activity weakens materially, those embedded rent gains could take longer to realize. There’s also interest rate sensitivity. Higher interest rates raise cost of debt and interest expense, thereby reducing investment spreads.

For Pfizer, execution risk is front and center. The pipeline needs to deliver. Cost cuts can only do so much. If new products underperform or face delays, the market’s skepticism will be justified. There’s also the broader issue of pricing pressure in pharmaceuticals. This appears to be a perennial issue as it relates to Medicare drug price negotiations.

Investor Takeaway

I don’t think either of these stocks is “cheap for no reason.” Industrial real estate is normalizing for Rexford and Pfizer is moving to bridge its portfolio to next-gen drugs.

But that’s exactly the kind of setup that favors patient value investors. Rexford offers a high-quality asset base with embedded growth. The market is underappreciating it because it’s focused on short-term leasing trends.

Pfizer offers a reset valuation with a still-diversified business. It has sufficient cash flow to bridge the gap to its next phase of growth. While neither story is perfect nor obvious, the best opportunities rarely feel comfortable at the start. Meanwhile, their pricing makes them attractive enough for a diversified value and income portfolio for potentially strong returns.

data.title: Phoenix Education Partners: Search Headwind Is A Real Risk To The Business (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for Phoenix Education Partners (PXED) previously, as I thought the valuation discount vs. peers was not warranted given it has similar growth, a better business model, and also higher margins. The reason I am downgrading to a hold today is because the near-term growth setup got less clean. The Google search algorithm disruption created a new headwind to student acquisition, and I think the market now wants proof that PXED can work through that before rewarding the stock with any meaningful re-rating.

2Q26 earnings review

PXED reported its Q2 2026 results two days ago. Total revenue was down slightly by 0.4% y/y to $222.5 million. While average enrollment went up 1.8% y/y to 82.6k, revenue per student fell 2.2% y/y. I don’t see this as a major problem since the decline came from a more normal student mix after tighter controls on suspicious or at-risk applicants. The bigger B2B mix also played a part in pushing pricing lower. As such, this was not a quarter where demand fell apart. In terms of operating metrics, retention improved to 76.6%, up ~500 bps y/y; B2B enrollment went up to 35% of total enrollment vs. 31% last year. Margins came in better than top-line performance. Gross margin was 52.7%, up from 52% in Q2 2025, adj. EBITDA margin was up 120 bps y/y to 15.7%, and adj. EPS came in at $0.58.

What changed and why I stay neutral

Back in my January writeup, I was bullish because the fundamentals were great: the business model looked better, margins looked structurally better in a mostly virtual setup, and regulatory risk also looked much lower. For a quick recap, my general idea was that once PXED cleaned up the student base, growth should become cleaner for investors to extrapolate. However, after 2Q26, I do not think that framing is as clean as I believed anymore. To be clear, the quality side of the story improved, but the growth side got messier.

There are operating numbers to support the qualitative aspect I noted. Retention improved to 76.6%, B2B mix increased to 35% of enrollment, bad debt came down, margins improved, and the board approved a $50 million repurchase program. Importantly, these points are linked. Better retention matters because students are staying in the program longer, and that showed up in lower bad debt expense. It also makes the B2B mix-shift story easier to digest for investors. For those that don’t follow PXED, B2B carries lower pricing, which puts pressure on the revenue per student metric, but the trade-off looks better when the total cost of acquiring these students is cheaper. An easy way to think of this is to look from a customer's (student in this case) lifetime value perspective [CLTV]. The stronger the retention, the higher the CLTV, and that makes the return-in-investment [ROI] better despite lower revenue per student metric. In other words, PXED is giving up some price, but getting better student quality and better margin economics.

Also, one thing to note is that the retention improvement is not due to luck. It was a result of implementing things like AI-assisted onboarding, predictive analytics, better early-course execution, etc. This is why I think the quality improvement is real.

The newer issue here is the change in Google’s search algorithms, and I think this is perhaps the biggest negative development for PXED. Remember that PXED is a digital-first business, so if prospective students are no longer finding or navigating to the University of Phoenix website through the usual search paths, the top of the marketing funnel gets weaker. That is exactly what happened during the January enrollment period, and it created a headwind to Q2 enrollment. To be clear, the immediate implication is not that demand disappeared. It is that conversion into actual enrollments slowed (since there are fewer leads), and we know the impact is real since management now expects revenue toward the low end of the guided range. The bear case narrative here is that if PXED is not able to fix this, it could be permanently disrupted, and that any potential for growth acceleration may be off the table for a while.

The good news is that this does not look catastrophic yet, and PXED does have some levers to respond. Management has already adjusted its content and digital marketing approach to fit the new search environment and is also still investing in marketing, employer partnerships, and AI to support enrollment growth. But I am not fully bullish that things could turn around anytime soon. There are a few reasons: (1) We do not really know the actions taken by management to “fit the new search environment." Is it just a matter of adjusting keywords? And what happens when AI search becomes more prominent? (2) Management only points to normalization beginning in March and entering 3Q26, and that does not imply full resolution. On top of that, 3Q26 also has a tougher y/y comp base because of the previous risk-free-period dynamic, which means even if search performance improves, the reported enrollment number may still look messy for another quarter.

Hence, to me, the issue is less about whether PXED understands the problem and more about whether it can show, over a few enrollment cycles, that student acquisition can return to a healthier pace under the new search setup. But, unfortunately, it is not something that can be proven in one month.

Valuation

I think there is still a chance that my previously modeled outcome could happen (mid-single-digit topline growth + flat adj. EBITDA margin + NTM EBITDA multiple expanding to 7x = ~$59 target share price), but I am cognizant that the debate is not whether PXED can achieve that. The recent results led me to believe the market focus is whether PXED can even achieve its FY2026 guidance and whether the new marketing-funnel headwind is going to structurally alter the business growth profile. There are green shoots to both elements. Retention did move up; and for the marketing funnel situation, there have been early signs of normalization in March. However, I believe the market wants more proof over the next few quarters, and for that reason, the stock is unlikely to see any form of major re-rating, in my view. As such, I am downgrading to hold for the near-term.

Conclusion

From some perspective, PXED fundamentals seem to have improved, but I think there are reasons to be cautious in the near-term. The issue is that student acquisition and revenue visibility are now less clear because of the search-funnel disruption. In my view, PXED needs a few more enrollment cycles to prove growth can be steady before the stock can rerate.

data.title: Commodity Strategies Diverge As Roll Yield Takes Over
data.description:
Not all commodity funds are built the same. Right now, that matters. The differences are doing more than showing up in performance. They are telling a story investors need to understand.

A rare and powerful market dynamic is unfolding.

Energy markets have been turned upside down by a historic supply disruption. A conflict-driven shutdown of Gulf oil production, estimated at nearly 9 million barrels per day, has driven crude oil futures into steep backwardation. As of April 7, front-month WTI is trading near $110 per barrel, while prices for delivery in late 2026 slope down toward the mid-$70s. This creates a powerful positive roll yield tailwind.

In Understanding the Components of Commodity Futures Returns, we looked at how spot price movement, collateral yield and roll yield drive commodity returns. We’re seeing that framework in action today, with roll yield now taking center stage.

When futures curves are backwardated, investors rolling expiring contracts sell high and buy low, earning a positive spread. History shows how consequential this can be. During the structurally backwardated energy markets of the 1970s and early 1980s, S&P Goldman Sachs Commodity Index (S&P GSCI) roll yield averaged 4.77% and 2.41% annualized, respectively. In contrast, when energy contango dominated the 2000s, that same figure turned sharply negative, to -8.25%.

The current environment is squarely in the first camp—and it is historically rare.

CMCI: Built for the Long Game

The UBS Constant Maturity Commodity Index (“CMCI Index”), the benchmark behind both the VanEck CMCI Commodity Strategy ETF (CMCI) and CM Commodity Index Fund, was designed with a specific, long-term view: commodity futures markets are in contango most of the time, and when in contango, front-month concentration is the most expensive place for investors to sit.

The index addresses this by spreading exposure across the futures curve, spreading positions three months to three years. Combined with broader commodity diversification, this approach mitigates the negative roll yield impact that has historically been the biggest drag on commodity returns over time.

The numbers bear this out.

Today’s Roll Yield vs. Long-Term Trends

Source: Bloomberg, VanEck. Data as of 3/31/2026. CMCI: UBS Bloomberg Constant Maturity Commodity Index (CMCITR) ; BCOM: Bloomberg Commodity Index; S&P GSCI: S&P Goldman Sachs Commodity Index. Annualized roll yield refers to the annualized return the amount of return generated from the rolling of a short-term futures contract into a longer-term contract and profits from the convergence of the futures price toward a higher spot or cash price. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Please see index definitions and other important disclosures at the end of this content. Fund performance current to the most recent month end is available by visiting vaneck.com or by calling 800.826.2333.

Why the CMCI Index Is Lagging—And Why That's Expected

The feature that makes this strategy a more resilient long-term holding is precisely what is causing it to lag in the current environment, when near-term backwardation is steep.

Spreading maturity exposure across the curve means the strategy holds some exposure in the longer-dated portion of the energy curve, parts of which is still in contango beyond 2027. At the same time, lower energy concentration relative to the S&P GSCI means it captures less of the sector generating the most positive roll yield today.

Neither of these is a flaw. It is a tradeoff.

This strategy was built to mitigate the persistent headwind of contango, not maximize returns during short-lived periods of extreme backwardation. This design has been right for the vast majority of the past two decades, and will be right again when this supply shock resolves and the curve normalizes.

In short: the CMCI strategy is a strategic all-weather commodity allocation. The S&P GSCI is a high-beta, energy-concentrated tactical instrument that may perform well in environments like today’s, but carries different risks across cycles. Investors who chose the CMCI approach for its volatility management, drawdown characteristics, and roll efficiency made a sound, well-reasoned decision — one that is temporarily in an unfavorable regime, not one that has been invalidated.

The Case for PIT: Tactical Flexibility Across Changing Regimes

For investors seeking to actively participate in environments like the current one, the VanEck Commodity Strategy ETF (PIT) offers a different approach. Unlike passive indices, PIT is not constrained to a fixed position on the curve. It can dynamically allocate across maturities and sectors, which means it can lean into front-month energy exposure when backwardation is steep and reposition as conditions evolve.

In a market where front-month energy backwardation is generating positive roll yield and longer-dated contracts remain in contango, that flexibility matters. An active manager can position explicitly to capture that spread. PIT returned 18.64% in March 2026 alone and 36.61% in Q1 2026, its strongest quarter since inception. That performance reflects the tactical advantage of being unconstrained, with the ability to lean into front-month energy exposure today and rotate as fundamentals evolve.

Two Different Paths for Commodity Investing

The current environment is a reminder that commodity investing involves not only what you own in different regimes, but also how you own it.

Roll yield can dominate outcomes depending on the shape of the futures curve, and different strategies are built to harness or mitigate that effect in varying ways.

  • VanEck CMCI Commodity Strategy ETF (CMCI) and CM Commodity Index Fund are designed to deliver a smoother long-term experience across typical market conditions.
  • VanEck Commodity Strategy ETF (PIT) provides the flexibility to adapt across regimes, seeking the strongest opportunities wherever they emerge.

These are complementary, not competing, approaches. Understanding how they differ and when those differences matter are essential to making informed allocation decisions in commodities today.
